title: Kochen-Specker nonlocal hidden variables must include time-ordering to allow for measurement independence of several agents
abstract: We consider an ontology, in which contextual nonlocal hidden variables are stored as pre-existing possibilities in a repository outside space-time; and in which the context can be chosen ``freely'' (measurement independence) by each agent, both in spacelike and timelike configurations. We show that, in Bell-type experiments involving several agents, for this ontology to be consistent, the context must include not only the measurements that can be performed, but also the time ordering of the choices of different agents.
summary: The paper shows that if one assumes a nonlocal hidden variable model where values are stored outside spacetime, the model must account for the time-ordering of measurements to prevent the loss of agent independence. This prevents 'circular signaling' and ensures that the choice of one agent does not pre-determine the freedom of another in timelike configurations. This result offers a way to maintain a single-world ontology while respecting the principle of free choice.
main_problem: The paper addresses the conflict between Kochen-Specker nonlocal hidden variables and the requirement of measurement independence (free choice) for multiple agents in Bell-type experiments.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that for a nonlocal ontology to remain consistent with agent independence, the measurement context must include the time-ordering of the agents' choices, not just the measurement settings.
method: The authors use a proof by contradiction involving signaling and a decomposition of multipartite quantum correlations into conditional probabilities based on an arrow-of-time polytope.
model_or_system: An 'All-Possible-Experiments' (APEX) ontology where nonlocal hidden variables are stored in a repository outside of spacetime, involving multiple agents (e.g., Alice and Bob) in spacelike and timelike configurations.
key_observables: Measurement settings (inputs), measurement outcomes, and the temporal sequence of measurement events.
important_parameters: Measurement independence (free choice), time-ordering of measurement events, and the signaling between agents.
main_assumptions: The existence of a repository of pre-existing possibilities outside spacetime, a single-world ontology, and a strong form of measurement independence.
figures_summary: Table 1 shows a nonlocal deterministic assignment for CHSH that allows signaling; Table 2 shows the upgraded context including time-ordering; Table 3 illustrates circular signaling.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the APEX ontology, identifies the signaling conflict in Bell-type experiments, proposes time-ordering as a solution to preserve measurement independence, and generalizes the result to multipartite systems using probability decomposition.
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental ontological perspective on the nature of nonlocality and the role of time in quantum correlations, which is relevant to the foundational aspects of quantum information and the interpretation of multipartite entanglement.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
